ocr.text: The table presents a comprehensive analysis of various inflammatory markers and fatty acid profiles in relation to hepatitis E
virus (HEV) infection, specifically focusing on data collected at 28 days post-infection (dpi). The metrics of interest include C-
reactive protein (CRP) and interleukin-6 (IL-6), both of which are pivotal indicators of inflammatory response. The correlation
coefficients indicated for these markers suggest a significant relationship with the presence of HEV RNA, as evidenced by the
positive-strand and negative-strand HEV RNA detection columns. Furthermore, the fatty acid composition, including omega-3
(n-3) and omega-6 (n-6) fatty acids, provides insight into the potential modulatory effects of dietary components on
inflammatory pathways during viral infections.
CRP  IL-6  TNF  ALA  EPA  DPA  DHA  n-3  LA  DGLA  AA  n-6
Days post-inoculation (DPI)  Groups  The number of positive rabbits’ ovaries for positive- and negative-strand HEV RNA detection
Positive-strand HEV RNA detection  Negative-strand HEV RNA detection
28 dpi  Experimental groups  1/4  0/4
Control groups  0/4  0/4
49 dpi  Experimental groups  2/4  2/4
Control groups  0/4  0/4
Dachau  Zülke et al. [11]  Charny et al. [47]  Weimann et al. [42]
Atypical resection  20 (46)  15 (28)  33 (48)  84 (50)
Segmental resection, resection of more than one segment  19 (44)  32 (60)  12 (18)  38 (22)
Hemihepatectomy  4 (9)  6 (11)  23 (34)  47 (28)
The experimental groups analyzed in this study indicate varying levels of inflammation and immune response, which may have
implications for therapeutic strategies targeting HEV. Overall, this table underscores the complex interplay between
inflammatory markers, fatty acid profiles, and viral load, contributing to our understanding of HEV pathophysiology.
